Preface

Based on various concerns, governmental authorities in low- and middle-income countries, as well as in some high-
income countries, often ban exportation of domestic human plasma, or do not have in place regulations and legislation
to guide the export of plasma. However, the World Health Organization (WHO) regards transfer of domestic plasma
across borders, when linked to provision in return of industrially manufactured plasma-derived medicinal products
(PDMPs), as a compelling national interest. This is especially so in settings where access to essential PDMPs is inadequate
to meet patient needs. This document was developed to inform ministries of health about the WHO policy as part of
a programme to improve global access to essential PDMPs. It discusses the existing barriers to exportation of plasma
and provides the rationale for policy decisions in favour of foreign processing (“fractionation”) of domestic plasma to
improve availability and affordability of PDMPs. It also recommends measures for overcoming barriers to cross-border
transfer of domestic plasma, which is defined as shipment of domestic plasma to a manufacturer in a foreign country
under an arrangement for “contract” or “toll” fractionation.

title: Chapter 1. Introduction
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Chapter 1. Introduction

One of the main challenges identified as hampering global efforts to address the current barriers to access to safe
blood and blood products is the lack of availability of plasma-derived medicinal products (PDMPs). The World Health
Organization (WHO) Action Framework to advance universal access to safe, effective and quality-assured blood products
2020–2023, proposed six strategic objectives (1). One of the outcomes of strategic objective three “Functioning and
efficiently managed blood services” is to increase the volume and quality of plasma available for PDMPs.

title: Introduction
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Introduction

Although there are many variations on the arrangement for plasma fractionation under such a foreign contract,
a common barrier is resistance to cross-border transfer of domestic plasma. This resistance is grounded in ethical,
economic and scientific concerns that can be addressed in a well-controlled national plasma programme.

The following chapters discuss the background and the reasons for resistance and propose solutions.

Development of the document

This document was developed based on identified issues predominantly in low- and middle-income countries regarding
underlying concerns on prohibitions against the cross-border transfer of domestic plasma and the existing barriers to
the exportation of domestic plasma to improve the availability and affordability of PDMPs. An initial virtual meeting
of a selected group of experts in transfusion medicine and plasma fractionation, WHO Regional Advisers for Blood
and Transplantation, and WHO headquarter Blood and Other Products of Human Origin staff, was initiated to discuss
these issues resulting in agreement on the need to develop policy guidance on the implementation of cross-border
transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products (PDMPs) was discussed and established.
The group identified the key topic areas and individual working groups were established to produce the initial text
for each of these areas. An editorial group was formed from the leaders of the individual working groups and the final
document developed through a reiterative process by the initial group before review by a number of WHO identified
external experts.

title: 2.1 PDMPs are needed everywhere, regardless of income
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section: 2.1 PDMPs are needed everywhere, regardless of income
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
2.1 PDMPs are needed everywhere, regardless of income

Industrially manufactured (“fractionated”) PDMPs, including coagulation factor concentrates FVIII and FIX and normal
immunoglobulin, are listed in the WHO Model List of Essential Medicines for adults and for children. These lists identify
the medicines essential to meet a population’s health care needs. A number of LMICs have little or no access to PDMPs,
primarily because of their very high commercial costs relative to the available resources, and the lack of plasma
collected in these regions or the inability to meet GMP requirements. The inability to provide these medicines has a
profoundly negative impact on the morbidity and mortality of people born with inherited disorders related to lack of,
or non-functional forms of plasma proteins. These include primary immunodeficiency, haemophilia A, haemophilia B,
von Willebrand Disease, alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, hereditary angioedema and other life-threatening rare diseases
of unknown (autoimmune or inflammatory) cause, such as idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, Kawasaki disease
and chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy. People with these conditions are found in all populations,
whether or not they are identified diagnostically within the health system.

title: 2.2 Manufacturing process of PDMPs
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
2.2 Manufacturing process of PDMPs

PDMPs are manufactured in a process that takes up to 12 months. Internationally recognized standards for preparation
of plasma for fractionation, such as those from WHO, should be followed (4). The process starts with the donation
of plasma. This may be plasma collected through apheresis or recovered plasma, which is plasma not needed for
transfusion, that is separated from a whole blood donation after the donation process is completed.

title: Background
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Background

are extracted. The separated, specific proteins are purified, virally-reduced and adjusted to the optimal composition,
concentration and dosage before becoming the finished therapeutic product. Each plasma donation, whether source or
recovered, is tracked throughout the process – from donation all the way to the final products. Each year approximately
130 source plasma donations are needed to treat one person with a primary immunodeficiency, 900 donations to
treat one person with alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency and 1200 donations to treat one person with haemophilia (5). For
economic reasons, a minimum annual volume of approximately >30 000 litres of quality-assured plasma is needed to
make contract fractionation worthwhile. However, the volume is subject to agreement with the fractionator.

title:
2.3 Previous and ongoing efforts to ensure access to PDMPs
in LMICs
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section:
2.3 Previous and ongoing efforts to ensure access to PDMPs
in LMICs
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
2.3 Previous and ongoing efforts to ensure access to PDMPs
in LMICs

The supply of PDMPs is lacking or restricted in many countries, and, over the years, many attempts have been made
to improve the situation. Some countries have made plans to rectify the lack of products by setting up domestic
fractionation facilities. However, very few countries have succeeded with such projects for various reasons. These
include lack of funding and resources, and lack of knowledge or basic infrastructure to perform the complete plasma-
to-final-product-process, such as that related to meeting high quality demands.

In 2009, under the “Achilles project” WHO recognized that insufficient production of plasma meeting standards for
industrial fractionation was a limiting factor in global access to PDMPs. WHO revitalized the Achilles project under the
“Action Framework to advance universal access to safe, effective and quality-assured blood products 2020–2023” and
published its Guidance on increasing supplies of plasma-derived medicinal products in low- and middle-income countries
through fractionation of domestic plasma in 2021 (1, 6). In this guidance, WHO recognized the value of a stepwise approach
in LMICs towards eventual access to PDMPs. These steps include:

the vitally important step of setting up a quality system of a sufficient level in the blood system, for collection

of plasma;

the possibility to start with local preparation, in quality-compliant blood centres, of safe plasma proteins including

virally-inactivated cryoprecipitates; and

most importantly for this guidance, the possibility to utilize domestically collected plasma for fractionation by

an international partner to achieve access to the PDMPs.

title:
2.3 Previous and ongoing efforts to ensure access to PDMPs
in LMICs
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section:
2.3 Previous and ongoing efforts to ensure access to PDMPs
in LMICs
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
All these steps need to be considered before any plans for investments in local fractionation are made. When local
capacity to implement GMP expands and when GMP is actually implemented, the volume of quality-assured plasma
potentially suitable for fractionation increases.

title: 2.4 Existing barriers to cross-border transfer of plasma
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section: 2.4 Existing barriers to cross-border transfer of plasma
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
2.4 Existing barriers to cross-border transfer of plasma

from a corresponding amount of non-domestic plasma. Subsequently, the PDMPs are imported with the appropriate
authorization for clinical use in that country.

In blood systems where transfer of plasma is a new step in the country’s handling of blood and plasma, there can be initial
resistance to the idea, often based on perceived ethical, economic or scientific concerns, or functional impediments.
Such resistance creates barriers to the use of plasma for further manufacturing outside a country’s borders (7)

Barriers to plasma transfer can be placed into two general categories: concerns of the country considering plasma
transfer, and strict requirements of the fractionator. However, such concerns can be addressed in a well-controlled
national plasma programme that includes a proper regulatory and legislative framework and appropriate local (national
or regional) policies, or through public–private partnerships.

Concerns of the country considering plasma transfer

Typical concerns of the country considering plasma transfer are:

lack of national regulation, policies or a legislative framework, including competent oversight by the responsible

authority, which would allow cross-border transfer or export of domestic plasma for this purpose;

economic considerations regarding allocation of resources across the health care system;

ethics of donation for plasma transfer;

concerns about donor health arising from the need to collect more plasma; and

lack of information or analysis of which model is most appropriate in the national context.

title: 2.4 Existing barriers to cross-border transfer of plasma
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section: 2.4 Existing barriers to cross-border transfer of plasma
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
These elements will be further explored in this document, which endeavours to provide evidence to alleviate those
concerns and provide a roadmap for building the required frameworks.

Barriers to acceptability by the fractionator

These barriers fall into two categories – inadequate volume and insufficient quality – as described in WHO’s 2021
Guidance on increasing supplies of plasma-derived medicinal products in low- and middle-income countries through
fractionation of domestic plasma (6)

The volume of plasma required is not defined and varies by fractionator, but it can be difficult to incorporate smaller
volumes of plasma as independent fractionation pools into the manufacturing schedule or to do so in a way that is
economically advantageous to the collecting country. Increasing the volume of plasma collected is generally required
of a blood system embarking on the process of plasma transfer.

Factors preventing the collection of adequate volumes include:

insufficient equipment capable of freezing within the specified time frame in addition to maintaining the cold

chain including plasma at temperatures of −20 °C and colder, depending on legislation;

inadequate transport infrastructure in the country, which would hinder or delay the movement of plasma to a

foreign fractionator;

fragmentation within the blood system, preventing the considerable costs of maintaining and certifying GMP

compliance from being shared among donation centres;

lack of harmonized national regulation, preventing plasma collected at individual centres from being pooled; and

focus on the significant upfront cost of initiating a plasmapheresis programme, which has been found to be a

title: 2.5 National experiences
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
2.5 National experiences

Based on WHO global survey data, 60 out of 108 countries, 34 of which are in the WHO African Region, reported annual
whole blood collection rates less than 10 donations per 1000 people for the year 2018. 1 Only a few African countries
are using the surplus plasma recovered from whole blood donations to produce much-needed PDMPs. Most African
countries have an established national blood policy in place. According to this policy the national blood establishments,
together with regional, satellite and mobile collection centres, are responsible for collecting, testing, component
separation, and supplying blood and blood components to both public and private health care facilities to meet patient
needs. Most of the blood establishments are wholly owned, financed and managed by the national government. Over
the past two decades, blood collections in Africa have increased significantly, in part due to international funding from
organizations such as the US President’s Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief, advertising and donor recruitment activities,
as well as guidelines and assistance from organizations such as WHO (9). To improve the quality and safety of blood
and blood products and to ensure compliance with international standards, many countries have sought accreditation
from independent organizations such as the African Society of Blood Transfusion (AfSBT) and the Association for the
Advancement of Blood and Biotherapies (AABB). There are numerous challenges hindering African countries from
supplying quality plasma for fractionation. These include:

Inadequate or noncompliance with quality management systems and GMP in accordance with WHO or other

title: 2.5 National experiences
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
internationally recognized guidelines: Quality management systems are well-documented; however, the
implementation and adherence to their policies is found to be lacking in practice. Similarly, GMP requirements are
often understood by staff, but due to inadequate resources, insufficient funding to replenish consumables, poor
facilities, lack of schedules for the repair and maintenance of equipment, and deficient procedures, requirements
are circumvented, thereby compromising GMP.

Absence of national policies or regulations allowing for the export of surplus plasma for fractionation into PDMPs:

The implementation of a regulation or policy can take several months or years as it requires input from multiple
stakeholders and is also dependent on the commitment and prioritization by government. Plasma and PDMPs are
not well understood by regulators and policy-makers, and this also delays the drafting of necessary regulations.

National regulatory authorities do not have the knowledge and experience necessary for inspecting and

regulating the national blood establishments and evaluating their compliance with international standards: The
national blood establishments rely on independent organizations such as the AfSBT or AABB – which comes at a
cost – to conduct accreditation assessments to ensure a minimum standard is achieved at all stages of the whole
blood collection process, namely, donor selection, whole blood collection, laboratory testing, blood component
separation, plasma freezing, labelling, storage and transport.

Limited financial support is available from government to purchase the equipment and consumables required

title: 2.5 National experiences
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
for blood collection and component separation into plasma products: Most blood establishments in LMICs are
fully funded by government and do not utilize any established form of cost recovery model. A full or partial
cost recovery model allows the blood establishment to sell blood and blood products to both state and private
health facilities at prices that cover the cost of blood collection and include a reasonable margin to ensure the
blood transfusion service is self-sustaining. Due to the numerous health priorities and limited financial resources
in LMICs, national governments are often unable to provide resources to increase whole blood collection, for
example, by supplying suitable collection bags to allow for component separation into plasma products and
advanced testing platforms to ensure the safety of blood and plasma.

High staff turnover, inadequate skills and training: Many blood transfusion services in LMICs have reported

high staff turnover, which results in staff shortages. Recruitment of adequately qualified and skilled staff is
also a challenge, especially in remote or rural areas. Due to high staff turnover and the necessity to recruit new
staff, knowledge transfer does not always take place and training of new staff may be delayed, which impacts
compliance with GMP requirements.

Poor transportation infrastructure: Plasma for fractionation needs to be collected, separated and stored at

title: 2.5 National experiences
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
temperatures colder than −20 °C within 24 hours to extract labile proteins and 72 hours to extract non-labile
proteins. These time requirements are critical to ensure that the plasma will be suitable for fractionation into the
appropriate PDMPs. However, in remote and rural areas where road transportation is not well established, delivery
of whole blood for separation and plasma freezing within the required timelines poses a significant challenge.

In countries that are landlocked and do not have easy access to seaports, transportation of plasma to international
fractionation facilities is a particular challenge as this increases the risk of temperature excursions.

Inadequate supply of basic amenities such as water and electricity: Availability of clean water and an uninterrupted

power supply is also a challenge in LMICs, especially in remote settings. Shortages of water and power will restrict
the usage of certain equipment, impact GMP compliance and adversely affect the quality of plasma collected.

title: Chapter 3. Donated plasma as a national resource
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section: Chapter 3. Donated plasma as a national resource
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Globally, patient needs for treatments with essential PDMPs including clotting factors and immunoglobulin G are not
being met. The World Federation for Hemophilia estimates that 70–80% of haemophilia patients, mostly in low-income
countries and LMICs, as defined by the World Bank (10), do not have access to adequate treatment (11, 12). Similarly, the
International Patient Organization for Primary Immune Deficiencies has reported that, worldwide, a high percentage
of patients with primary immunodeficiency do not receive appropriate treatments. These deficiencies in diagnosis
and care, which cause otherwise avoidable suffering and mortality, result from failures to ensure the availability and
affordability of PDMPs. Lack of appropriate care is further aggravated by failures to diagnose the inherited and acquired
disorders that can be treated with these products, often because health care personnel are aware that these treatments
will not be available. Validated small-scale preparation of pathogen-¬reduced cryoprecipitate and immunoglobulin in
GMP-compliant blood establishments can provide a first and temporary source of safe plasma protein products for some
patients (13). However, sufficient access to PDMPs as the products of choice requires a national commitment to ensuring
their availability and affordability, either through humanitarian donations or direct purchasing. Fractionation of domestic
plasma under a contract with an internationally accredited foreign manufacturer (contract or toll fractionation) offers
an alternative or complementary mechanism to assure a stable supply of PDMPs, in some cases at a net cost lower than
that of direct purchasing of products with equivalent characteristics. Through fractionation under a foreign contract,
the economic value of plasma as a critical raw material for manufacture of PDMPs is realized. Wastage of plasma that
can be fractionated negates its medical and economic value.

title: Chapter 3. Donated plasma as a national resource
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section: Chapter 3. Donated plasma as a national resource
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
A premise of good governance is to maximize the health and fitness of the population. Thus, it follows that providing
patient access to essential PDMPs should be a public health goal. The recognition that domestic plasma is a valuable
raw material for the manufacture of medically essential PDMPs is central to the goal of achieving a supply of these
products sufficient to meet patient needs. This applies to plasma recovered from whole blood, plasma obtained as
a by-product of cellular apheresis (“concurrent plasma”) and to plasma prepared directly through plasmapheresis.
Discarding “surplus” plasma that is not needed clinically for transfusion or missing the opportunity to generate surplus
plasma components from donated whole blood because of the absence of, or a low rate of, separation, therefore
contribute to insufficiency of PDMPs in settings where the cost of commercially purchasing them is prohibitive and
where this domestic plasma could be fractionated. This is wasteful economically since the value of the surplus plasma

Donated plasma as a national resource

goes unrealized. Additionally, discarding surplus plasma is questionable ethically since it devalues the gifted donation
and deprives needy patients of potential access to quality-of-life-enhancing and often life-saving PDMPs.

A government-supported programme that recognizes the medical and economic value of domestic plasma as a resource
for manufacture of PDMPs can help to mitigate unmet patient needs by assuring preparation, storage and transport
of donated plasma that meets quality standards and a sufficient aggregate volume for foreign fractionation. This can
be achieved through establishment of a national blood and plasma programme that:

sets and enforces standards for plasma preparation;

title: Chapter 3. Donated plasma as a national resource
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section: Chapter 3. Donated plasma as a national resource
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
coordinates the movement of plasma domestically and across borders;

negotiates contracts for foreign fractionation; and

authorizes importation of PDMPs made with domestic plasma or made with foreign plasma but returned in

exchange for transferred domestic plasma.

Furthermore, utilization of domestic plasma to secure a reliable supply of PDMPs can avert supply disruptions that
can arise from external factors affecting the international availability of plasma for fractionation and the commercial
marketing of PDMPs. Viewed in this way, the supply of domestic plasma suitable for fractionation can be recognized as
a strategic national resource relevant to public health, which it is critical to protect and maintain, analogous to sources
of energy or clean water (14).

title: Chapter 4. Ethical concerns regarding plasma
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Chapter 4. Ethical concerns regarding plasma

exportation

Various objections to plasma exportation by relevant national decision-makers arise from ethical concerns. These can
be addressed through a well-managed programme of cross-border transfer of domestic plasma. Ethics are defined
as the “moral principles that govern a person’s behaviour or the conducting of an activity” (15). In the context of this
document, the “activity” is the provision of blood and plasma products derived from donated blood and plasma.
Classical principles of bioethics (16) applicable to this activity include:

respect for autonomy: respecting the self-determination and choices of patients and participants;

nonmaleficence: avoiding or minimizing harm to others;

beneficence: promoting or doing good for others; and

justice: ensuring fairness and equality in the distribution of benefits and burdens.

Consistent with these principles, harms to blood and plasma donors must be avoided and benefits to patients must
be realized. From the ethical perspective, suboptimal use of donated blood and plasma (failures of respect for donor
autonomy and of beneficence), and adverse impacts of plasma exportation on the health system (failures of beneficence
and justice) are perceived overtly or intuitively as unacceptable consequences of plasma exportation.

These adverse outcomes, which can and should be avoided, can be understood to arise from exportation of domestic
plasma that is not linked to benefits to patients and to the health system in which the donations are made. Ethical
acceptance of cross-border transfer of domestic plasma therefore depends on:

assurance of donor safety;

optimal use of donated blood and plasma to benefit patients, for example, through linkage to access to essential

title: Chapter 4. Ethical concerns regarding plasma
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
PDMPs in public contracts, or use of plasma that would otherwise be discarded; and

Ethical concerns regarding plasma

exportation

management of the blood and plasma system to prevent negative social and economic impacts that might

undermine access to safe blood for transfusion.

In this context, donors should be informed that PDMPs manufactured from their altruistic donations will become
economically valued products that have costs.

Moreover, it follows that moral duties exist to avoid exploitation of donors and to make optimal use of this donated
human substance for the benefit of both:

the patients who depend on the medicines derived from it; and

the health system in which the donation is made (17)

These ethically driven objectives can be achieved under nationally or regionally organized plasma programmes that
link plasma exportation to importation of PDMPs. Such programmes would have regulatory safeguards in place to
protect donor health, to assure quality of the PDMPs and to provide patients with fair access to the product.

Current barriers to cross-border domestic plasma transfer undermine the interest in making optimal use of donated
blood and plasma for two main reasons:

Countries lacking the capacity to fractionate plasma domestically are deprived of the possibility of fractionation

agreements that promote enhancements in the domestic blood and plasma collection systems. Upgrading of
the blood systems has been taking place in several countries with ambitions to fractionate.

Countries are unable to utilize and export fractions from the fractionation process that are surplus to their needs.

Being able to do so would allow products made from these fractions to be supplied in areas that otherwise lack
the capacity to access them.

title: Chapter 4. Ethical concerns regarding plasma
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
The development of a suitable framework for plasma exports on a regional level, overseen by a common regulatory
framework, would allow the fractionation of plasma by contract. This would enable supplies to countries lacking
fractionation capacity or that individually produce an insufficient volume of plasma to permit non-domestic fractionation.
This is only possible if blood establishments conform to the relevant international standards. For this reason, access to
plasma products obtained through these arrangements will also enhance the quality of the blood service in countries
contributing plasma in general.

An adequate supply of plasma for fractionation can only be ensured through the provision of source plasma, augmenting
the supply of recovered plasma. The frequency of donation by plasmapheresis presents an ethical challenge because
the health of the donor needs to be protected from protein depletion (18). Thus, the frequency and volume of donation
should be supported by scientific data to demonstrate adequate protection of donor safety and plasma quality. When
these principles are applied, there is no ethical impediment to widening the donor pool through plasmapheresis if
adequate standards are in place. On the other hand, collecting blood in excess of red cell needs in order to supply plasma
for fractionation, as has been done in the past, raises clear ethical issues related to the wastage of unnecessarily donated
red cells and this should be discouraged.

title: Chapter 4. Ethical concerns regarding plasma
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Whole blood collection with preparation of recovered plasma is mainly performed by public sectors, whereas plasma
collection by plasmapheresis is typically performed by the private sector, but increasingly also by the public sector
in parallel to whole blood collection. Establishing a balance between these coexisting systems is crucial. Otherwise,
the supply of blood and blood components for transfusion may be influenced adversely. The national authority with
responsibility for blood and plasma collection must be tasked with providing guidance to both sectors when it comes
to donor recruitment and donor retention. Assuring financial neutrality in compensation or payment of source plasma
donors avoids a potentially negative impact on public willingness to donate whole blood by limiting an incentive that
might otherwise favour donation of source plasma over donation of whole blood. Financial neutrality also protects source
plasma donors from exploitation and prevents the preferential recruitment of economically disadvantaged individuals.

title: Chapter 5. Legal and policy concerns
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Chapter 5. Legal and policy concerns

Besides the ethical concerns discussed in Chapter 4 and the economic aspects discussed in Chapter 7, there are also
legal concerns related to cross-border transfer of domestic plasma and the subsequent import of PDMPs. These apply
to the following:

Plasma needs to be of sufficient quality to be accepted for fractionation.

Plasma needs to be transported to the fractionator, in a different country.

PDMPs are to be imported into the plasma-supplying country.

For the first and the second aspects, the specifications of the fractionator need to be adhered to during donation,
manufacture, procurement, storage and transport of the plasma. This entails compliance with GMP as laid down, for
example, in the WHO guidelines on good manufacturing practices for blood establishments (19). These requirements need
oversight by the relevant national regulatory agencies.

For the regulatory agencies to be able to fulfil this duty, they need the legal mandate to license and inspect the blood
establishments, and, if necessary, to sanction them in cases of misconduct. Another important aspect is traceability
between the single donations and the resulting PDMPs to allow follow-up of cases of potential transmission of infectious
bloodborne diseases. For this, data about the donations need to be stored safely and to be transferred to the fractionator,
for example, in a plasma master file or equivalent documentation. This requirement should be addressed in a national
haemovigilance and pharmacovigilance system that allows for the surveillance of the entire transfusion chain (20)

title: Chapter 5. Legal and policy concerns
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
The plasma has to be transported under standardized temperature-controlled conditions, which should be validated
and monitored. In the case of cross-border transport, it is necessary to clearly define who is responsible for this control
on both sides of the border. Since the transport over the border constitutes an export of goods, corresponding legal
requirements need to be in place or to be established to permit this cross-border transport. Moreover, the customs
personnel need to be educated and trained accordingly.

Legal and policy concerns

In some countries, a legal framework is required to issue permission for plasma export (as well as for plasma import)
as it is a human-sourced material. This permission is sometimes issued by the authorities per shipment. From its side,
the authority of the country where fractionation takes place must approve the plasma and the centres in which the
plasma has been collected, so that the plasma can be allowed to enter the receiving country.

title: Chapter 5. Legal and policy concerns
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
For the third aspect, the legal requirements for the import of the product, including Good Delivery Practices, need
to be fulfilled. As for all medicinal products, legal controls are in place to prevent importation of compromised or
counterfeit PDMPs. The PDMPs will need to have a current MA in the country to which they are imported. Where the
imported PDMPs include non-domestic plasma, a scientific and regulatory assessment has to be made to determine
the acceptability of the final products based on comparable risks of the differently sourced plasmas. If a country lacks
regulatory standards for authorization of PDMPs, they may be adapted from regulatory systems in more advanced
settings. It is also important to have sufficient personnel available who are adequately trained to perform these tasks.
Where the MA of PDMPs is still a challenge, a short-term solution might be to base the national authorization on reliance
on the MAs of non-domestic internationally recognized agencies, for example, WHO-listed authorities. Since the long-
term aim is to supply the country with sufficient PDMPs for the domestic patients who need them, a sole importation
in response to the demand of a physician for products to treat a single patient is not a viable national option, although
legal requirements may permit this under exceptional circumstances of individual medical need.

title: Chapter 5. Legal and policy concerns
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
In some countries, PDMPs are subject to lot release by a national competent authority (NCA). These will most likely
come from the NCA of the country or region where the marketing authority holder (MAH) of the PDMPs is located. If
the plasma-supplying country requires lot release certificates, it needs to be clear in which form these are to be issued.
For example, the European lot release certificates of the Official Medicines Control Laboratory (OMCL) network are
legally valid only within the European Union and the European Economic Area (EU/EEA). However, increasing numbers
of countries outside the EU/EEA recognize the value of the Official Control Authority Batch Release (OCABR) certificate
and are requesting such a certificate from the manufacturer. Indeed, for these recipient countries, adoption of the EU
OCABR certificate is also an added guarantee of the quality of the batches marketed (20, 21). While adoption of the EU
OCABR is a possibility, documentation of regulatory requirements for batch release can be met through use of other
comparable formats. Regulations and standards need to be adopted mutually between the authorities, and their
respective duties should be clearly defined. Among others, the main topics for clarification include duties relating to
initial and follow-up GMP inspection, change and deviation control, and complaint and recall procedures.

title: Chapter 5. Legal and policy concerns
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
A detailed quality agreement is required covering all aspects of the contract for plasma and PDMPs. This includes defined
roles and responsibilities, use of specified donor screening test kits and facilities, look-back procedures, and provision
of epidemiological data. In some cases, this agreement will need to be approved by the local authorities. Establishment
of post-marketing surveillance procedures with clear definitions of roles and responsibilities is also important.

These aspects should ideally be addressed in a regulatory framework that is under the control of the NCAs for medicines
of both the countries that are providing the plasma (and receiving the PDMPs), and the country where the fractionation
and manufacturing takes place. Both agencies need clarity about each other’s responsibilities to allow a smooth flow
of goods and information.

Therefore, to facilitate the transport of plasma across the border to the fractionator and the reimportation of the
PDMPs, an adequate regulatory framework must be developed. There are already good examples of how this can
work in regions of the developing world. In sub-Saharan Africa, where the supply of the population with PDMPs is still
low despite the waste of plasma, fruitful interactions between the national blood transfusion services and a regional

title: Chapter 5. Legal and policy concerns
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
fractionator are helping to identify the gaps in the regulatory system and to support their remediation. Similar activities
are taking place in south-east Asia. Another way forward is to use the WHO Global Benchmarking Tool plus Blood (22) for
a self-assessment to test the status of the national regulatory system. Based on the results of the analyses, insufficient
regulatory frameworks can be developed and/or strengthened and adapted to the specific situation in each country.
This may, for example, be accomplished by making use of the experiences of countries that already export plasma
and import PDMPs in return, for example, in the form of collaborations or twinning projects. Also, existing regional
organizations may be used or may be formed allowing countries with different competences to complement each
other to form a network of reliance. One such example is the ZaZiBoNa-Network for medicines registration (23). Such
regional networks may also blend their plasma to reach a sufficient pool size for fractionation.

Countries seeking to collect plasma in a way that facilitates acceptance by fractionators can, as an important first step,
adopt internationally recognized standards like those of the Pharmaceutical Inspection Cooperation Scheme (PIC/S)
(24) and WHO guidance (4). PIC/S Guidance documents stipulate all quality measures from donation to GMP. PIC/S good
practice guidance for blood establishments and hospital blood banks (PE 005) was revised in 2021 and is a key document
to adopt in local regulations. By adopting PIC/S standards or comparable internationally recognized standards, countries
can comply with the requirements of regulatory authorities in countries where fractionation facilities are operated, as
well as those specified by the fractionators.

title: Chapter 6. Strategies to protect the health of
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section: Chapter 6. Strategies to protect the health of
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Chapter 6. Strategies to protect the health of

plasma donors

Ensuring the health and well-being of blood and plasma donors is crucial in all countries and is a particular concern in
LMICs. The approach taken should not only put an emphasis on ethical standards but can also help mitigate concerns
of the public regarding the potential “exploitation” of donors.

title: 6.1 Considerations for whole blood donors
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
6.1 Considerations for whole blood donors

In environments with limited resources, ensuring the availability of blood and blood products for blood transfusion in
patients is often a challenge. Where there is a shortage of blood products, efforts at blood donor recruitment should
aim to increase whole blood donations that are also used to prepare recovered plasma.

Where plasma for fractionation is recovered from whole blood, or is a by-product of blood component apheresis, the
donor is also the plasma donor, and the donor’s health should be protected by the same means and measures and in
line with practices for protecting whole blood donors. Selection and testing of whole blood donors are essential to
prevent risks to patients receiving blood transfusions and, equally important, to protect blood donors from adverse
outcomes of a donation. Before being selected, donors should fill in a donor questionnaire and undergo a physical
examination (pulse, blood pressure and temperature). Laboratory testing, depending on jurisdiction, should include
haemoglobin, blood typing and testing for evidence of transfusion-transmitted infectious diseases. Additional donor
testing for ferritin level depends on the jurisdiction.

Measures to protect the donor include:

screening to ensure general health and freedom from certain recent or chronic infections, disorders and exposures;

determination of a minimum acceptable body weight;

limitations to the volume and frequency of blood collections;

Strategies to protect the health

of plasma donors

performance of the blood collection by well-trained personnel using sterile equipment and aseptic techniques;

clinical oversight to monitor for potential hypotensive, allergic or other adverse reactions.

title: 6.1 Considerations for whole blood donors
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Additionally, donors should be informed of abnormal findings and referred for follow-up medical evaluations and
management when appropriate.

An important health focus for whole blood donors is on maintaining the iron store. To address this, ferritin testing can
be added to haemoglobin testing as a precautionary measure for the protection of donors. Low iron stores in a donor
should lead to an individualized donor interval being set, for example, less frequent donation might be recommended.
To protect donors, the donation frequency must be clearly regulated, as is done by the safety rules for substances of
human origin (SoHo regulations) in the EU (25, 26) or the regulations of the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA)
(27), among others.

In summary, in situations when only recovered plasma is collected, plasma donors’ health will be protected mainly in
the same way as for any whole blood donor.

title:
6.2 Considerations for plasmapheresis programmes and
source plasma donors
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section:
6.2 Considerations for plasmapheresis programmes and
source plasma donors
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.

title:
6.2 Considerations for plasmapheresis programmes and
source plasma donors
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section:
6.2 Considerations for plasmapheresis programmes and
source plasma donors
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
compensation for time and inconvenience, consistent with financial neutrality;

obligation for donors to be screened and plasma to be tested for infectious diseases on two separate occasions

before donated plasma can be manufactured into PDMPs.

Within their well-regulated environment, high-quality plasma collection centres impose a strong safety framework to
ensure that plasma donors are and remain healthy. To protect their health, potential donors are required to undergo
a series of standard health screenings:

health history questionnaire (regular medications, recent medical procedures, medical conditions and risks);

physical examination and vital signs (temperature, blood pressure, pulse, weight, examination of the skin of

the arms);

blood and plasma tests (for example, haematocrit, protein testing, haemoglobin and tests for infectious

diseases) (29).

People with a medical condition or other factors that may impact their ability to safely donate plasma are not accepted
as donors. However, if the health condition or other factors that would impact a person’s ability to safely donate plasma
has changed or been resolved, it may be possible for them to donate plasma at a later time. Therefore, the donor may
be asked to return after a specific temporary deferral period to be reconsidered as a donor.

The plasmapheresis process (having blood removed, plasma separated, and red blood cells, white blood cells and
platelets returned to the donor’s body) is monitored throughout by trained and licensed medical professionals
(phlebotomists).

Plasma donors receive oral or intravenous fluids at the end of each donation to address volume loss (30–32).

In some countries, donors are further monitored for a brief period after their donation.

title:
6.2 Considerations for plasmapheresis programmes and
source plasma donors
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section:
6.2 Considerations for plasmapheresis programmes and
source plasma donors
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Additional measures such as those established in the International Quality Plasma Programme (IQPP) of the Plasma
Protein Therapeutics Association (PPTA) should be considered. These include independent, third-party evaluation and
recognition of a plasma donation centre’s adherence to global industry standards for donor health protection and
management, which complement regulatory requirements (33). The IQPP includes measures to:

support plasma donors with information on protecting their health and leading a healthy lifestyle;

enable plasma to be replenished quickly by providing fluids (34); and

A stringent regulatory environment, a comprehensive safety framework in centres and adherence to voluntary industry
standards mean that adverse effects of plasmapheresis are rare. Collection of records of immediate donor adverse
events as part of the plasma programme, combined with more data on long-term health, will contribute to safe and
sustainable plasma collection (36, 37).

title: 6.3 Haemovigilance
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
6.3 Haemovigilance

Haemovigilance for donors, or donor vigilance, is a system to register adverse events. Importantly, since adverse events
and reactions are infrequently seen, there is a need to record and analyse them. The adverse effects of blood donation
are an important indicator for determining the efficacy of blood transfusion safety measures. Such measures include
standards for acceptance of the volume of donated blood or plasma in relation to the body mass and environmental
or social conditions, for example, ambient temperature, recent fasting, etc. Also, many countries with limited resources
are situated in high-temperature zones and their population may have a lower mean body weight than in Europe and
North America, which could potentially lead to a higher risk of adverse effects.

Standardized donor-vigilance data on regular donors should be recorded and evaluated to detect long-term undesirable
side-effects (38, 39)

title: 7.1 Factors to consider
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
7.1 Factors to consider

When establishing a sustainable and efficient system for the fractionation of domestic plasma, it is necessary to explore
the alternative arrangements to find the one that best suits the unique needs and constraints of the local health
care system and patients’ needs. 2 The decision to adopt a specific arrangement should be informed by a thorough
understanding of various critical factors that need to be examined to determine the feasibility and effectiveness of
the fractionation programme.

The selection of an arrangement for the fractionation of domestic plasma thus requires a balanced consideration of
various factors:

National requirements

The specific requirements and resources of the local health care system are linked to the unique characteristics

and needs of the local health care infrastructure. These include logistics, storage and distribution capabilities.
All these factors must be taken into account to ensure that the fractionated plasma products are accessible and
beneficial to the intended recipients nationwide.

The feasibility of having access to and being able to assign trained and skilled personnel representing the key

stakeholders to lead and carry out the process of engaging with potential fractionators should be explored.
Competencies needed include:

knowledge of local plasma collection and the related quality and GMP demands;

commercial and contractual knowledge for the contract; and

Alternative arrangements and economic

considerations for fractionation

of domestic plasma

knowledge of the local distribution of, and market for, medicinal products.

If domestic fractionation is an eventual goal, competencies to plan for and operate a domestic facility can be included.

title: 7.1 Factors to consider
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
General regulatory aspects concerning plasma and PDMPs

The maturity of the domestic regulatory system – its robustness and developmental stage – plays a pivotal role

in ensuring that the quality of the plasma, the fractionation process and the resulting PDMPs meet international
safety and quality standards.

The regulatory requirements in the country of the fractionator need to allow for use of the domestic plasma from

the plasma-providing country.

The ability to add, or not, domestic plasma to the fractionator’s plasma master file (or meet similar regulatory

requirements for collecting and ensuring the quality of plasma for fractionation). If this is possible, the PDMPs
made from this plasma can be approved for sale by the plasma fractionator.

Allowance for potential mixing of the domestic plasma with plasma from other sources in product preparation.

Mixing may result in marketing of such PDMPs in other countries. This also means that the country supplying the
plasma would receive PDMPs made from plasma collected in other areas. Such an arrangement requires careful
consideration of compatibility and regulatory compliance.

Product (PDMP)-related

The PDMPs imported from the fractionator need to have MA that meets the national requirements.

The pharmacovigilance system for imported products needs to be in place according to local regulations.

The suitability of the dosage form of PDMPs to be administered is crucial for their efficacy and acceptance within

the local health care context (for example, capacity to manufacture formulations for paediatric use). The selected
fractionation arrangement should, therefore, align as well as possible with the dosage and pharmaceutical form
preferences and needs of the local population in the importing country.

title: 7.1 Factors to consider
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
The plasma product range, in terms of the diversity of PDMPs that can be produced and distributed, is essential

for comprehensively addressing the health needs of the community. The fractionation arrangement should
facilitate a product range that matches the local demand for PDMPs, although this can also be achieved in a
stepwise fashion. It is important to acknowledge that sometimes additional imported PDMPs will be necessary
to meet the needs of certain patients for specific PDMPs.

Technical and quality considerations

The volume of plasma available directly impacts the choice of fractionation arrangement, as it influences the

scale and feasibility of operations of many global fractionators, and the range of PDMPs and dosage forms that
can be made from the plasma.

The contract with a fractionator will include some form of quality agreement where the details of the plasma for

fractionation and many other quality-related aspects can be described in detail.

Economic considerations

Toll or contract manufacturing: the cost of fractionation as well as the economic implications of different

fractionation methodologies should be evaluated to ensure that the chosen approach is financially viable and
sustainable in the long term. It is important to inquire into the overall yield of the different PDMPs as this can
have an economic impact, favouring processes with a high yield. More details on consideration of the economic
impacts of a toll or contract manufacturing arrangement are discussed as an example in section 7. 3.

Where arrangements including sales of plasma are linked to importation of PDMPs, the prices of these need to

be agreed.

Fluctuating currency exchange rates and tariffs (as seen during the COVID-19 crisis when there was a substantial

title: 7.1 Factors to consider
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
increase in the cost of transportation) have to be considered in any arrangement. Fluctuating rates can affect the
overall cost of the export–import cycle, and restrictions on tariffs or trade can add unforeseen costs to the process.

title: 7.2 Possible alternative arrangements
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
7.2 Possible alternative arrangements

A multifaceted approach, encompassing both the strategic factors influencing the choice of fractionation arrangements
and the potential models that could be implemented, can be adopted. Potential alternative arrangements to ensure
a strategic supply of PDMPs encompass:

Contract or toll fractionation. This refers to the situation where domestic plasma meeting the criteria for

fractionation – as verified by national regulatory authorities – is collected and shipped for processing by a
fractionator, typically located abroad. The resulting PDMPs are subsequently returned to the country of origin
under contractual terms. This model is suitable for countries lacking domestic fractionation capacity. Under
such a contract, and assuming compliance with regulatory requirements, part of the intermediate fractions or
of the PDMPs produced, which are not needed domestically to meet the local needs of the country supplying
the plasma, may be kept by the plasma fractionator and distributed in other countries or regions. A contract or
toll manufacturing arrangement can also be designed as an intermediary step prior to a transfer of technology
from the fractionator to a suitable partner in the country seeking to invest in domestic fractionation of plasma
and supply of PDMPs.

Sales of plasma to a fractionator clearly linked to purchase of PDMPs from the same fractionator, within the same

title: 7.2 Possible alternative arrangements
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
contract. In this scenario, the purchased PDMPs can be manufactured from the same plasma as is sold to the
manufacturer, but there are also arrangements where the plasma is sold to the fractionator and the PDMPs are
also made from plasma (in volumes that have been agreed upon) from other approved plasma sources. This
type of contract has many of the same elements as the above-mentioned contract/toll manufacturing one, but
a different economic structure.

Public–private partnerships. These partnerships are collaborations between governments and private entities.

They are designed to organize a contract plasma fractionation programme or to establish local fractionation

facilities in a system attempting to combine public oversight and private efficiency. Under such arrangements,
a joint venture between the fractionator and the national ministry of health may open a number of plasma
collection centres. These may be operated by the fractionator and collect plasma to be used for fractionation
outside the country. Later, when possible, fractionation may be undertaken domestically, for provision of PDMPs
to the population.

Regional fractionation hubs: This is a cooperative model where several countries pool resources and plasma,

demonstrated to be of equivalent quality and safety, to establish a shared fractionation facility. This would
optimize resources, training requirements, and economies of scale and facilitate access to PDMPs across borders.
Such a model would require investment in a fractionation facility for this purpose.

In-country fractionation facilities. This is where a domestic infrastructure ensures local fractionation capabilities,

title: 7.2 Possible alternative arrangements
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
enabling domestic control over the production and supply of at least parts of the PDMPs. Establishing a domestic
facility, when justified, is usually best achieved after a ramp-up phase of contract fractionation that allows fast-
track access to needed PDMPs and can serve as a critical phase of increasing plasma production and training of
local operators.

Hybrid models: a tailored approach may combine elements of the above-mentioned strategies to meet specific

domestic or regional needs and capabilities.

Any arrangement for plasma transfer aimed at production of PDMPs needs dedicated and knowledgeable teams and
suitable stakeholders for both partners. Public–private partnerships, where collaborations between governments and
private entities are designed to organize a contract plasma fractionation programme or to establish local fractionation
facilities in a system attempting to combine public oversight and private efficiency, may be one option.

The optimal arrangement for the fractionation of domestic plasma is thus contingent upon a comprehensive evaluation
of both the strategic factors at play and the practical models available. By carefully considering the regulatory, economic
and health care landscape, alongside the potential fractionation strategies, local stakeholders, in partnership with
foreign entities and organizations, can devise a plan that maximizes the use of domestic plasma resources. This plan
should enhance the availability, accessibility and quality of PDMPs, under appropriate control of the national health
care system, thereby addressing the health needs of the local population effectively and sustainably.

title:
7.3 Example of economic considerations in a contract or toll
fractionation agreement
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section:
7.3 Example of economic considerations in a contract or toll
fractionation agreement
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
7.3 Example of economic considerations in a contract or toll
fractionation agreement

In many cases of contract or toll plasma fractionation, the plasma is not actually sold to the fractionator. Instead, the
arrangement involves a service contract where the fractionator processes the plasma into PDMPs and the manufactured
PDMPs are returned to the plasma supplier. In this setup, invoicing does not cover the cost of the plasma itself but
instead is based on the production services required to convert plasma into PDMPs. The economic terms for this service,
which should be clearly defined in a legal and technical contract, can be based on a fee per litre of plasma fractionated
with an agreed and guaranteed yield of the end-products. Alternatively, it may be based on a fee per unit (IU or g) of
each PDMP actually returned to the plasma supplier. The lack of invoicing for the plasma itself focuses the practical
economic exchanges at the time of plasma exportation towards service costs, logistics and quality assurance, also

playing a role in the financial viability of a contract plasma fractionation programme. The invoicing for the fractionation
service is the main critical component in the overall economics of the arrangement. It must be accurately calculated to
include not just the direct costs of fractionation but also the indirect costs related to the plasma fractionator’s profit,
and costs of maintaining the plasma’s quality throughout the entire cold chain and transportation process.

title:
7.3 Example of economic considerations in a contract or toll
fractionation agreement
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section:
7.3 Example of economic considerations in a contract or toll
fractionation agreement
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Since, in a traditional plasma fractionation agreement, the plasma is not being sold, the primary financial exchanges
when plasma is exported encompass the cost of shipping the plasma to the fractionator and of shipping the processed
products back to the country of origin. One primary cost element in plasma exportation for contract or toll fractionation
is associated with maintaining the cold chain. It is necessary to ensure and record that plasma remains at below-
zero temperatures during its storage in a centralized warehouse (typically −20 °C or colder) in the country of plasma
collection and throughout transit (typically −18 to −20 °C or colder), regardless of the distance to the fractionation
facility. Specialized refrigerated containers and vehicles are required, which adds to the initial capital and operational
costs. Depending upon the locations of the plasma suppliers and the fractionator, it may be feasible to use trucks for
shorter distances and relatively small plasma volumes. Shipping containers via boat are usually selected for overseas
transportation and for larger volumes but need careful timing and port clearances. Air transport may be selected for
small volumes or in specific circumstances and is the most expensive option. It should be kept in mind that any delays
or inefficiencies in the selected transport system may compromise the plasma quality and incur financial losses.

Finally, once the plasma has been fractionated, the end-products must be shipped back under conditions in accordance
with suitable Good Distribution Practices for medicinal products. This includes temperatures compatible with the
demonstrated stability of the PDMPs to guarantee their safety and efficacy. This adds another layer of cost.

title:
7.3 Example of economic considerations in a contract or toll
fractionation agreement
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section:
7.3 Example of economic considerations in a contract or toll
fractionation agreement
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
In summary, the economic concerns pertaining to the exportation of plasma for contract fractionation include the
contractual fee for processing plasma into PDMPs, the cost for cold chain maintenance during storage, transportation
logistics and compliance with trade regulations.

title: Chapter 8. Conclusion and recommendations on
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Chapter 8. Conclusion and recommendations on

cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to

obtain PDMPs

In LMICs, absent or insufficient access to essential PDMPs, including clotting factor concentrates, polyvalent
immunoglobulin G and albumin, compromises the health and survival of patients with inherited clotting disorders,
primary immunodeficiency and acute massive haemorrhage. Likewise, although not extensively addressed in this
document, limited access to hyperimmune immunoglobulins derived from plasma with a high concentration of
specific antibodies compromises patient care. In particular, insufficient access to anti-D immunoglobulin and hepatitis B
immunoglobulin compromises prevention of haemolytic disease of the newborn and of hepatitis B virus transmission,
respectively. At the same time, there is considerable wastage of domestic plasma that could be used to manufacture
PDMPs if it met the quality standards for fractionation. In this context, an organized national or regional programme for
fractionation of domestic plasma under a contract with an established foreign manufacturer for reciprocal importation
of PDMPs made wholly or in part from that plasma (contract or toll fractionation) is recognized as an effective strategy
to improve the availability of essential PDMPs to meet identified patient needs.

title: Chapter 8. Conclusion and recommendations on
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Implementation of contract or toll fractionation requires a comprehensive domestic blood programme including
regulatory oversight to assure an adequate and stable supply of plasma. The plasma also needs to meet internationally
recognized standards of GMP for blood and plasma collection, testing, processing, storage and transport. Ultimately,
contract or toll fractionation necessitates cross-border transfer of domestic plasma that is linked contractually to
regulatorily authorized importation of PDMPs provided by the manufacturer. In addition to the inherent quality, safety and
logistic issues related to management of the transferred plasma, ethical, scientific, legal, regulatory and economic concerns
can and should be addressed within the programme to assure its societal acceptance and economic sustainability.

In consideration of the need to improve access to PDMPs in many LMICs and some HICs, WHO recommends removal
of national or regional legal and/or policy barriers to cross-border transfer of domestic plasma that is designated for
contract or toll fractionation under the following conditions:

A competent national governmental authority provides oversight to assure that GMPs are followed at all levels

of the blood and plasma system.

title:
Conclusion and recommendations
on cross-border transfer of domestic
source: Implementing cross-border transfer of domestic plasma to obtain plasma-derived medicinal products policy guidance .pdf
section:
Conclusion and recommendations
on cross-border transfer of domestic
citation: WHO. Implementing Cross-Border Transfer of Domestic Plasma. World Health Organization.
content:
Conclusion and recommendations
on cross-border transfer of domestic

plasma to obtain PDMPs

Regulatory safeguards are in place to assure the health and safety of donors, including limitations on the volume

and frequency of collection of blood and plasma.

Legal requirements backed by adequate enforcement mechanisms are in place to prevent importation of

compromised or counterfeit PDMPs.

If PDMPs imported under the programme involve use of non-domestic plasma solely or in a mixture with domestic

plasma, a scientific and regulatory assessment has been made of the acceptability of the final products, based
on comparable risks of the differently sourced plasmas.

Where a programme of plasmapheresis exists to generate source plasma, measures are in place to avoid

compensation in excess of financial neutrality.

The value of the plasma that is exported as a raw material partly offsets the costs of the finished PDMPs.

Donors are informed that PDMPS manufactured from their donations that have costs of preparation and

procurement will become economically valued products for some patients’ health and survival.

A national programme exists to assure equitable patient access to the PDMPs obtained through contract or

toll fractionation.

Cost reimbursement for procurement of PDMPs under contract or toll fractionation is managed under a

sustainable national or regional budgetary commitment.
